scenario: Halcyon Logistics writes a custom Go client to send 4000 requests per day to a Camunda 8.8 Zeebe cluster. The team has installed grpcurl, obtained an OAuth access token, and now needs the contract artifact so the language stack can encode and decode messages for the gateway endpoint.
question: Which artifact must a custom gRPC client obtain from the Zeebe repository to invoke the Camunda 8.8 gateway?

options.text: Download the gateway.proto buffer file from the Zeebe repository and import it when invoking gRPC methods on the gateway endpoint
Download an OpenAPI specification from the Zeebe repository and feed it into a code generator to produce gRPC stubs for the gateway
Download a precompiled gRPC stub library from the Zeebe repository and link it into the application without any proto buffer file
Download a WSDL contract from the Zeebe repository and load it at runtime to discover the available gRPC service methods of Zeebe

a.text: Correct. The documented procedure has the client fetch gateway.proto from the camunda/zeebe repository and pass it as the proto file to the gRPC invocation against the Zeebe gateway.

b.text: Incorrect. The build-your-own-client steps reference a gRPC proto buffer file, not an OpenAPI specification, as the contract for the Zeebe gateway calls.

c.text: Incorrect. The docs explicitly require the gateway.proto file as input to the gRPC tooling; no precompiled stub library is provided in place of the proto.

d.text: Incorrect. The Zeebe gateway exposes gRPC services described by gateway.proto, not by a WSDL contract; WSDL is a SOAP service description format.

explanation: The build-your-own-client guide instructs developers to download gateway.proto from the camunda/zeebe repository and pass it to grpcurl (or any gRPC library) to invoke methods such as gateway_protocol.Gateway/Topology on the Zeebe endpoint.
